titleInfo.title: Literary Construction of Buddhist Sacred Places: The Record of Mt. Lu by Chen Shunyu

mods.abstract: Record of Mount Lu (Lu Shan ji 廬山記 compiled by Chen Shunyu 陳舜愈 around 1072. 937: "literary essays describing the mountains are combined with collections of verses and inscriptions." Conclusion 962–963: Constructing Mount Lu as a Buddhist sacred site by the presence of a missionary monk, in this case Huiyuan 慧遠 (334–416). Two strategies: Dragon Spring Temple: negotiation with local deities, “a place with extraordinary natural features marking passages with supernatural realms” Donglinsi: “bringing distinctly Buddhist sacred objects from outside the mountain, thus establishing the presence of the Buddha and his teaching in this site”. “The famous Aśoka image that miraculously responded to Huiyuan’s prayer to come to Mt. Lu was over time transformed into Mañjuśrī who lives on the mountain, in the same way this bodhisattva is said to reside at Mt. Wutai.” 943: This account of Huiyuan’s arrival and his temples draws heavily on Huijiao’s biography of Huiyuan in the Biographies of Eminent Monks. Huiyuan founded Dragon Spring Temple, and also Donglingsi Temple 東林寺, where the Terrace of the Buddha’s Shadow and a miraculous Asoka image are located. 945: “The Donglingsi temple is presented not as a place where a local deity appeared but as a place where extraordinary Buddhist objects were brought from outside and placed.” 947: “Donglinsi is here presented as a Buddhist sacred place by means of a rhetorical strategy very different from that used in presenting the Dragon Spring temple.”
